nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving, warm savannah streams and pools in the Llanos, the vast grassland plains of Venezuela and Colombia. Habitat is often heavily vegetated with leaf litter and a sandy substrate, and water levels fluctuate dramatically with the seasons.

decorPreferences: flat rocks or smooth stones for spawning sites
caves or terracotta pots as shelters

diet.notes: The German Blue Ram is a benthic feeder — one that forages along the bottom — and tends to accept live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, and grindal worm readily. Sinking micropellets and high-quality flake are also accepted. Offering a varied diet is particularly important when conditioning a pair for breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (Cardinal Tetra, Rummynose Tetra)
avoidWith: fast-moving boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
species requiring harder or cooler water

summary: The German Blue Ram (Mikrogeophagus ramirezi) is a small, vibrantly colored dwarf cichlid from the Venezuelan and Colombian Llanos, prized for its striking appearance and devoted biparental care. It requires warm, soft, and acidic water and does best as a bonded pair. Most trade stock is commercially farmed, which tends to affect hardiness.
careNotes:
Temperature is the most common husbandry failure point — many community tanks run too cool (22-25°C) for this species. Rams generally do best at 27-30°C; sustained temperatures below 26°C have been associated with increased disease susceptibility and shortened lifespan. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.5-6.8, gH under 10) is strongly preferred, especially for breeding and long-term health. Harder water is tolerated short-term by some individuals but is generally associated with reduced vigour.

Commercially farmed stock — the vast majority sold in the trade, much of it from Asian fish farms — is widely reported by hobbyists and specialist sources to have significantly compressed lifespans, often 1-2 years rather than the 3-4 achievable with higher-quality stock, along with elevated susceptibility to disease, particularly fish tuberculosis (Mycobacterium), columnaris, and parasitic infections. Wild-caught or European specialist-bred stock tends to be considerably hardier. Selectively bred colour morphs such as the Electric Blue Ram, Gold Ram, and Longfin Ram are generally considered more delicate still.

The Bolivian Ram (Mikrogeophagus altispinosus) is widely regarded as a hardier, cooler-water alternative (22-26°C), better suited to standard community tank temperatures and less experienced keepers.

Rams are pair-bonded substrate spawners. A compatible pair is essential — incompatible fish will fight. Each breeding pair needs a territory; in larger tanks a second pair can be kept if sight lines are broken. Adults become territorial near spawning sites. Despite their reputation as a poor community fish, they can coexist well with small, non-competitive tankmates in a well-planted tank with adequate space.

A biotope-style setup with fine sand, driftwood, leaf litter, and dense planting significantly reduces stress and tends to bring out the best colouration and behaviour. Strong water flow should be avoided — gentle circulation at around 4-5 times the tank volume per hour is preferred. The species has been documented to recognise individual keepers and may display to them.
breedingNotes: The German Blue Ram is a biparental substrate spawner. Pairs typically choose a flat stone, broad leaf, or cleared sandy patch as a spawning site. Clutches tend to consist of 150-300 eggs. Incubation has been reported at 2-3 days at 28-30°C, followed by a free-swimming stage within 5-7 days. Both parents share guarding duties. Raising the eggs can be challenging, as they are prone to fungal infection, particularly in harder or less pristine water. Soft, slightly acidic water with a low bacterial load gives the best hatch rates. First foods for the fry are infusoria or commercial fry powder, transitioning to Artemia nauplii as they grow. Commercial farm-bred pairs sometimes consume their first few spawns before settling into reliable parental behaviour.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature, tank size, diet, and tankmate guidance. Size cited as 2-2.5 inches (5-6 cm); minimum tank 20 gal (76L) for a pair.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichliformes confirmed), native range, water parameters (27-30°C, pH 5.0-6.0, dH 5-12), and max size (4.2 cm SL). Trade/display sizes larger due to including fins and caudal extension.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (5-7.6 cm), tank size (20 gal / 76L), temperature (26-30°C), pH (5.0-7.0), gH (6-14 dGH). Noted disease susceptibility to fish tuberculosis and ich. Listed lifespan as 2-3 years (vs Aquarium Co-Op 4 years; averaged to 2-4 range).

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner whose eggs are prone to fungal infection, particularly in harder or less pristine water. Fry require infusoria or commercial fry powder initially, then Artemia nauplii. Commercial stock pairs may consume early spawns before settling into reliable parental behaviour. Best results are generally achieved in soft, acidic water with temperatures above 28°C.
